author: @Flashahol

The beauty about this is, should you use the UPS charger, it will maintain your battery and you can forget about checking the charge regularly. 
HOWEVER, this charging method will not work well with AGM batteries as they need a much quicker charge...

author: @johnbrizendine7716
text:
The downside for these is that they do not have any charging real charging algorithm, and so does not ever go into "balance" mode and eventually the battery will sulfide to death.  I fixed that by having a solar controller on the battery while it is connected to the APC UPS.  The cheaper UPS backups will not work that way, but the APC UPS absolutely loves it!!!

I have a few different ones that I use as inverters and two that I use as actual UPS backups on my freezer and servers in my server rack.  
I have a few of the APC UPS backups rated in multiple KW of energy that are pure sinewave units on my servers in my basement and have solar and solar charge controller providing the full algorithm of battery maintenance charging connected directly to the batteries and the APC UPS and it works out perfectly.  
It took me a bit of research to figure this out, lead acid batteries like to be charged up to 14 volts for a short time every 24 hours in order to keep them in optimal working condition.  It seemed like the batteries would only last a few months at a time just connected to the APC UPS and then would get sulfated to the point they stop working, especially if I used large lead acid batteries.

author: @piconano
text:
On APC UPS you can disable the annoying beeper, using their RJ45->USB cable and software that comes with each unit. 
I desoldered mine, before discovering there was that feature 😔

author: @piconano
text:
If you notice the heat sinks they use for the mosfets inside, you will see big blocks of aluminum instead of typical teat sinks. 
There for, you can not use these inverters full time duty without modification to the cooling system.
They did that because it's cheaper and no fan is used. 
When the 2 batteries (24V system) on the APC800 died, I didn't want to buy new batteries, when I had 2x 100Ah boat batteries that had their good life elsewhere. 
It has worked great for the past 2 years. 
I placed the batteries inside a Rubbermaid container, and I extended the wires so that the UPS is now living on the closed container top. 
in my testing 20W was the max I could draw continuously without thermal shutdown occurs. 
I could modify the heatsinks, but there are riveted to the mosfets and pcb and a lot of work. I have a 400W and 1000W car inverters.
In a blackout, I can leech off the big 100A batteries and I'll have 2 full ones all the time, thanks to the UPS.

author: @TrollFaceTheMan
text:
The problem is with 12v and even very small loads the power losses become very high very quickly in the wiring if you try and say run a house off it.

 Also 12G wire can handle 20amps of power only. So that means at 12v your 12g wire will be good for a mere 240w.

 Which will also be heating up the wire significantly in the process.

 And honestly be closer to about 200w if you don't want your breaker nuisance tripping.

replies.author: @Zakardis
replies.text: Lithium batteries are more sensitive to being charged at too high a voltage (they can catch on fire). So they need a safety circuit, usually a temperature sensor on a battery pack, and a timer or sensor to shut off charging when the battery is full. Even lead acid batteries shouldn't be trickle charged for long periods of time. For a wind system you'd probably want to charge a capacitor bank, then charge the battery from that.

replies.author: @Zakardis
replies.text: If the original battery is bad, and you hook it up to a good battery in parallel, it'll fry the bad battery and probably the new one too. Same thing if you have a bank of batteries in parallel, if one battery fails, the other batteries are likely to fail from the bad one.

author: @TheBlackadder-Edmund
text: Not sure how accurate the following points are, as I have not tested, other points I have heard to take into consideration could be: 1-Run half the rated VAs (so if 400 VAs with 250Watts then try to not exceed 125Watts for longer use) as they may not be designed to run long times at the 250Watts. 2-UPS are used indoors with sealed batteries, when using a car battery beware of storage location (i.e. not inside home) and gas production from the battery (may need a place with air ventilation)

author: @mfr58
text: From experience, 12volts is a bit low for whole house distribution. I'd say 24volt is the minimum nominal rail to use. Note also that most electrical accessories are not rated for DC switching (possible arcing issues)and circuit protection can be difficult as dangerous heating can occur in a fault before it is cleared by a fuse or breaker, because the voltage is so low. Also fuses do have significant voltage  drop (at extra low voltage) at high currents. Having said that I do think there is potential for DC extra low voltage micro-grids in home and office. Power Over Ethernet (POE) is one approach to this and new standards for such distribution of power have been created.

author: @yepyep3402
text: In addition to the other warnings going around, I'd also caution that many UPS's arn't duty cycle rated for longer uptimes from larger batteries- the docs specifically advise against it.  The inverter circuitry, transformers and others are dramatically different inside the different models.   The higher end models (1000VA and greater) tend to not have an issue with it, but the desktop 500-750VA range units definitely can.   The power coming off the inverter of many of these desktop style models is also quite noisy and can cause problems with sensitive loads.

author: @theonlywoody2shoes
text:
HAZARD WARNING: Most of these APC UPS units tie the DC battery connection to the mid point of the AC input (transformer). You can check this with a meter from AC ground to either battery DC terminal. Here in the U.K. you will typically see 110v AC. With the original battery sealed inside the UPS this isn’t an issue, but if using an external battery the battery terminals will be referenced to mains voltage and therefore present a potential hazard.

Please check with a meter to ensure the DC battery terminals are not referenced to mains (AC input and/or inverted AC output) before using this configuration.

replies.author: @johnbrizendine7716
replies.text:
APC brand inverters are not like that, from what I can see.  That particular one is one I use and I also use the multiple KW rated rack mounted ones as well and they are isolated.  You are correct, if you don't spend the extra for a good quality UPS, you will get what you pay for.

The downside for them is that they do not have any charging real charging algorithm, and so does not ever go into "balance" mode and eventually the battery will sulfide to death.  I fixed that by having a solar controller on the battery while it is connected to the APC UPS.  The cheaper UPS backups will not work that way, but the APC UPS absolutely loves it!!!

author: @woodlandsdel
text: Good idea, but get a better UPS. Look at the underside at the warning label where the harmonic distortion is listed at 45 percent! Connect your oscilloscope to understand how bad the waveform is.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text:
Depends what you are powering. These things are usually intended to power computers for a short time - just enough time to save your documents. Computer power supplies do not care about the waveform at all. Many of them will happily run off of DC.

But if you try to run anything with an induction motor off this UPS, even just a little desk fan, then you will have problems. That's not the intended application for the UPS.

replies.author: @woodlandsdel
replies.text:
@vylbird8014 UPSs are “intended to power computers for a SHORT time”. Extending the battery capacity is intended to extend the run time.


Induction motors are not the only thing that the crappy waveform will harm. Even a DC power supply will have a reduced lifespan by the sharply rising and falling AC voltage. If you are going to spend money on a good UPS battery, first spend money on a good pure-sine UPS. They can be bought used cheap with bad internal batteries.

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text: Damn, I had a friend who used to listen to Motorhead, and I don’t think their distortion was that high!

author: @idahobob
text:
Though the years I've made many "Uber-UPS" similar to what Robert has done.
A couple things that I found out are don't use Flooded batteries, and make sure they can stay cool when running. 
Flooded Car and Deep Cycle batteries tend to "boil off" under the constant charging. The original are usually "Sealed Gel" lead acid but I don't recommend big one of those for your Uber-UPS.
AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat) batteries works the best, and doesn't emit gas, vapor, etc. They cost more but are well worth it.

On the cooling, remember that these consumer UPS's are designed to run for 10-15min, not the 2 to 4 hr they will with a larger battery. I haven't found a convenient way of adding more cooling that comes on when needed, but rather just oversize the inverter by quite limiting the load. An Uber-UPS will run for a very long time running laptop, a router, and charge phones rather than a whole computer or some other large load.

Last one I built was for a friend and his CPAP machine. I had a couple 1200VA units, and we put a 100Ah AGM on it, and it runs his CPAP for several nights on a charge.

It also takea about a week to recharge.

If you recharge via Generator, use the 12VDC output to charge the battery at 6-8A rather than the Mains, as it will only charge a large faction of an amp, and you'll run out of gas in the generator before you get a noticeable charge.

Last, if your UPS doesn't have a display on it. there are cheep LED meters you can add to your system to see the voltage and state of charge. Don't worry about them running all the time, the UPS will easily run them and charge the battery.

author: @3D_Printing
text: Some say the bigger battery's may give out gas???

replies.author: @cryptickcryptick2241
replies.text: yes, All lead acid batteries can give off gas. A small amount of this happens normally, under certain circumstances batteries can give off a lot of gas. Fun fact, in World War 2, submarines ran on lead acid batteries. Explosions form hydrogen gas given off from the batteries could sink the submarines. Fortunately, for one battery in a home, this is a limited risk, but something people need to be aware of.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Easy to hook up a solar array to keep the batteries charged if you parallel a lot of batteries. As far as the UPS goes, you need to be careful because a lot of the 250 to 500 watt models (even some larger models) are in fact modified sine wave inverter systems. You can get a 3000 watt pure sine wave inverter cheaper than you can get six 400 watt UPS's. As long as you run an inverter under its rated continual load it will last. My setup is 2 solar arrays on MPPT charge controllers managing 1000 mAh batteries. This runs the 3000 watt pure sine wave inverter that on most days runs half the house appliances which is a max of 2000 watts for about 10+ hours. This is why I want to add in wind turbines so I have night time solutions. My lights run on 12v DC 24/7.

author: @gshingles
text: I have done this for a while and yes, it works very well. I have an old 1600VA running on a couple of 120AH deep cycle gel cells. Nearly all of the small size UPSes I have seen have been 24V systems, so it's unusual for me to see a 12V one. That system has gone off a couple of times over five years or so and I haven't noticed any performance issues with the charging each time, even though it is probably charging solidly for days. From memory it does get a bit warm after a long outage, so some cooling over the charging circuit probably wouldn't hurt. I recently did another one but forgot to nobble the beeper while I had the case open :)

author: @dremaboy777
text:
This video was very useful. 
Thanks.

author: @islandsedition
text: Could you bypass the AC-in to plug in a variable current like your turbine?

author: @CrAzYDr1veR
text: To this day i don't understand why i cracked a fet doing the same thing to my old apc ups

replies.author: @yoda-be.at1746
replies.text: It could be turn on surges. I've suffered the same thing. The large transformers create a peak amp draw when being turned on by batteries, when connected to the mains, the flux doesn't collapse when being switched to battery power. I'm trying to find some sort of surge protector as mine runs from l-ion batteries which are even harder on the FETs...

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
I'm beginning to suspect you are psychic. My friend who lives in Lanzarote, asked me about this very thing yesterday (several years ago I helped her with a moderate UPS (marine batteries) as mains power was a bit fickle at times).
Thanks for the video and explanations.

author: @angelusmendez5084
text:
That's really interesting, just what I needed. Thanks very much! 
Awesome as always 👏👏👏

author: @intelligenttinkering
text: Great video mate. am I right in thinking in a time of a power outage you could flip your main breaker off and plug that directly into your plug socket making sure you turn most items off so you could just run your lights around your home ?

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text:
Not a good way to do that due to the risk of someone accidentally connecting to the mains lines.
If the power was out, you’ll be sending voltage out the mains, possibly where a lineman is working on an “inactive” down line.
When the power comes back on, it won’t be in phase with your inverter and could destroy it in a spectacular fashion.

Big Clive did a video on special switches for handling this, and they weren’t expensive.

author: @chuckles1808
text: I happened to have bought a UPS for this exact reason (and I wanted some lead dioxide plates for chemical reasons). Great video as usual.

author: @gkyahoo
text:
If you connect a car battery 10 times  bigger in capacity and you use the ups under load. Chances are the unit will burn itself because it does not has cooling capabilities, 
Reason is because it is designed to run at full current only for a few minutes, like to give you time to turn off a server properly
I run a apc ups from a computer room at home that has the xl designation, setup to connect extra batteries from factory and big fans , I can run a washing machine and vacuum the home at the same time

replies.author: @woodlandsdel
replies.text: I have multiple XL units, one is 48V 2700W powered by my golf cart battery bank. They are bulletproof.

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text: Got any extras?  😉

replies.author: @woodlandsdel
replies.text: @firstmkb I watch eBay for cheap APC XL units. Easy to spot by the rear battery connector.

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text: @woodlandsdel thanks!

author: @Gizmoautismo
text: Great video explaining the basics of the topic to everyone. I myself have worked with upses for a good few years now and i repaired a 6kVA online UPS and im making a custom voltage regulator to charge it's batteries from solar panels. It is definitely the most cost effective way of getting mains for home powering

replies.author: @TheKlink
replies.text: any chance of a vid?

replies.author: @rhiantaylor3446
replies.text: I think it is important to have a clear view of the loads you want to protect and the design  differences for larger UPS. I recently purchased a used 5000va/4kw UPS and discovered that the inverter operated its 20x 12V batteries as a series string - a 240v "battery". Not easy to upgrade when compared with - say - a 48v battery.

replies.author: @Gizmoautismo
replies.text: @TheKlink when i finish my exams i plan on recording what i do, so maybe soon-ish ill make a video :)

replies.author: @Gizmoautismo
replies.text: @rhiantaylor3446 indeed, mine runs on 15 12V batteries (so 90s cell configuration) so that is the biggest challenge to charge the array externally, but it is definitely doable if you have a few solar panels laying around :D

replies.author: @TheKlink
replies.text: @Gizmoautismo could be the start of something massive!!

author: @huarwe8797
text: We get charged for the electricity we consume when we don't actually consume.. the same way a water wheel does not consume the stream.

author: @scantrain5007
text: Many thanks for this! Apart from the longer runtime by bigger battery another aspect: The internal battery might 'spit out' the acid and destroy the electronics. An external battery solve this problem

replies.author: @plainedgedsaw1694
replies.text: They're using SLA's, those shouldn't leak unless something goes wrong.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Well we were waiting for the obvious .....the connection of that blue and yellow flower power for a battery charger lol.

author: @andymorgan2950
text: Enjoyed the vid! I have a larger APC SmartUPS 900 with knackered batteries. Can the Lead Acid battery in a UPS easily be replaced by a Lifepo4 battery? That would be more useful, longer runtime for alot less weight. Can it be done by using a BMS for the Lifepo4?

replies.author: @Gizmoautismo
replies.text: Yup! You can even use normal liions (with appriopriate BMS of course), but i recommend adding some buffer capacitors parallel to the rectifier output to smooth out some weird peaks that can often occur with the bms charger circuitry.

replies.author: @yoda-be.at1746
replies.text: I run an APC inverter from a 12kwh l-ion battery. So the answer is yes 😊

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text:
I can’t swear this is true for LiFePO4 batteries, but…
If you charge and discharge lipo batteries using lead-acid parameters, you will destroy them fairly quickly.  The discharge is the worst, which is why lipo devices have a cutoff for low battery.

author: @vossierebel
text: Thanks... I needed that!!👍😁

author: @1mremington
text: Great suggestion on running a home on DC!,  EMF from AC lines can be quite unhealthy.  Converting to AC at the load, which requires it, reduces this dramatically.  Many appliances do this in reverse (AC to DC).   If cars can operate on DC, so can a home!

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: EMF from AC lines does nothing to humans at all. Nothing unhealthy about it. Nothing healthy either. This has been extensively studied. Unless you are dealing with very high power levels (ie, standing next to a high-power radio transmitter, or in front of a damaged microwave oven), it does nothing.

replies.author: @1mremington
replies.text: @vylbird8014 What if there is high frequency noise on the line, say from switch-mode power supply?  Has this been extensively studied?  The human body is quite sensitive to electric and magnetic fields.

replies.author: @vylbird8014
replies.text: @1mremington Yes, it's been studied. Everything from DC electrical fields up to microwave. The human body is /not/ sensitive to electric or magnetic fields, at all. They do nothing, up until the intensity where you start seeing burns from heating.

replies.author: @1mremington
replies.text: ​@vylbird8014 Just last year the U.S. Supreme Court ruled, "The Commission [FCC] failed to provide a reasoned explanation for its determination that its guidelines [used by industry for decades] adequately protect against the harmful effects of exposure to radiofrequency radiation unrelated to cancer."  The WHO has classified cell phone radiation as a Group 2b carcinogen, due to brain tumors being associated with cell phone use.

author: @bjohnson1104
text: On TG_privat comment. I started to do this very project last month. I planned to add a fan to keep it cool. It turns out these smaller units (mine is a 750) do not have heat sinks with fins but something called a heat soak. A solid block of metal made to absorb heat for a limited time and then be given time to cool off. I wasn't sure that increased air flow would help in this case so I abandoned it for now.

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text: try a water cooler from a cpu this might move the heat for you and allow you to finish this project.

replies.author: @bjohnson1104
replies.text: @rayg436Thanks for the suggestion, I like the idea. But there's too many spread around the board. It doesn't make it impossible. It fails on effort for benefit.

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text: Thanks - I had never heard of a “heat soak”!  Talk about purposeful design limitations!

author: @tg_privat
text: Isn't there also the problem with overheating. These are ment to run only for a few minutes from battery. Is it not an issue?

author: @____________________________.x
text:
In theory you can run a PC off ~340Vdc, because inside the computers PSU it's just going through a bridge rectifier, and that doesn't care if it's AC or DC.
So you could have a stack of car batteries in series and do the same thing.
It's also more efficient to convert 12Vdc to 340Vdc, than it is to convert 12Vdc to a choppy 240Vac sine wave and then back to 5Vdc again...
Maybe you could try it? 😸

replies.author: @Omegaman1969
replies.text: It would work but be highly dangerous 😊 no protection at that voltage and you could end up dead very easily.

replies.author: @____________________________.x
replies.text: @Omegaman1969 240Vac might technically be more dangerous?

replies.author: @Omegaman1969
replies.text:
@____________________________.x Than 340VDC? No way , you would have RCD or an RCBO on your AC supply for shock protection. Try grabbing hold of 340VDC and let me know how you get on 🤣

I worked with a guy once when I was installing large computer room UPS machines. He wasn’t concentrating when connecting the large battery bank and grabbed hold of the last cable to be bolted to the battery ..,he flew across the room and landed on his toolbox , he was very lucky to have lived, just a burn line down his neck and arm.

replies.author: @____________________________.x
replies.text: @Omegaman1969 I was just thinking that AC tends to lock the hand onto the cable and kill you, whereas DC tends to throw you off. Just my experience in getting zapped with both, I try not to touch either. Apparently a type B RCD offers the same protection for DC circuits

replies.author: @Omegaman1969
replies.text: @____________________________.x Actually DC causing one jerk movement which throws you away from it, AC will freeze you onto the source.  AC is 3 times more dangerous than DC given the same voltage and current. I'm not saying DC isn't dangerous, it is. The problem is the body acts like a capacitor and lets AC through and blocks DC to a certain extent.

author: @onkelnb
text: I always thought that a UPS could be a smart way to bypass regulations. If you want to feed your Solar Power into the mains grid, there are lots of regulations. But a hacked UPS that would simply be plugged in between mains and any device would not feed any power into mains and so the regulations would not apply.  The battery would be charged with Solar power, the device would consume power from the battery and then there should be a clever hack possible that lets the device fall back to mains uninterrupted when the battery is empty.

replies.author: @fritanke2318
replies.text: Over here there is a litle known regulation on battery/dc low voltage installations that can draw 200VA at any time. I'm not sure if it is a national or EU regulation. So basically any installation that can draw 17A from a 12V battery must be done by a certified technician working for a certified company. Obviously all and every hifi entusiast in this country (Norway) is in breach with this regulation.

author: @FireFliesIlluminateMe
text: Very cool! You might need to upgrade the cooling if you’re using them continuously.

replies.author: @Gizmoautismo
replies.text: Indeed, some UPSes are pretty prone to overheating. I have a few of them and the first one i used as backup power was overheating after around 15 minutes.

replies.author: @firstmkb
replies.text: @Gizmoautismo did you just add a cooling fan for inverter mode, or something more?

author: @D-B-Cooper
text: I had a really big one from a mainframe computer. Put solar panels and charge controller and deep cycle batteries on it. Was rated for 20 amps. Lasted a couple of years and died. I just got an inverter and still have my solar generator.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: If it just 'died' suspect any electrolytic caps, then the DC/AC 'transistors' (they are often mosfets) as they can get a bit touchy about even small wrong polarity spikes on the gates.

author: @knickebien1966
text: How does one remove the DC/AC converter for an application that uses 12V DC rather than line current (e.g. home network routers, switches, modems, etc)? Seems wasteful to convert AC to DC for battery charging, convert that back to AC so a wall wart can convert it back to DC again.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Just run leads direct from the battery (in-line fused).

replies.author: @Gizmoautismo
replies.text: If you have devices that run on dc then you can either plug them directly into an appropriate voltage battery / power source skipping their normal ac adapters OR if you expect the input voltage to be unstable (for example while charging or your device needed a different dc voltage) you could also use a dc-dc buck/boost converter. They are cheap, efficient and easy to make them yourself if you have some soldering and design skills.

replies.author: @knickebien1966
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 That will not charge the battery and I only want to use the battery when the AC is out. I am describing an AC input UPS that has DC outputs.

replies.author: @woodlandsdel
replies.text: Search for a Belkin Residential Gateway BU3DC001-12V. They can be found used for cheap and have a regulated 12V output for your sensitive electronics.
